## 📌 Project Overview

> 💬 **What is this project?**
> You are a **data analyst at a public health research organization**. You have been given health records of 500 individuals categorized by gender, age groups, lifestyle habits (smoking, exercise), and diagnosed diseases. Your task is to use **pure statistical techniques** — no Machine Learning — to derive judgements about significant factors affecting disease occurrence.

- 🏥 **Domain** — Public Health & Epidemiology
- 👥 **Dataset Size** — 500 synthetic health records, 15 fields
- 🚫 **No Machine Learning** — 100% classical statistics only
- 🧪 **Tests Performed** — Z-Test, T-Test, Chi-Square, ANOVA, Correlation
- 🏫 **Institute** — Red & White Skill Education
- ⏱️ **Duration** — 6 Hours (Theory + Practical)

---

## 🎯 Project Objectives

- 📌 Apply **inferential statistics** to evaluate multiple hypotheses from a real-world-style health dataset
- 📌 Test **theoretical understanding** through short conceptual notes with visual diagrams
- 📌 Perform **hypothesis testing** using z-test, t-test, chi-square, and ANOVA
- 📌 Compute **confidence intervals** and interpret critical values and p-values
- 📌 Analyse **covariance and correlation** between continuous health variables
- 📌 Derive clear **Accept / Reject H₀** decisions for every test with interpretation
- 📌 Present all results through **clean infographic visuals** embedded in the notebook

---

## ✨ Features

| ✅ Feature | 📝 Description |
|-----------|---------------|
| 🤖 **AI-Generated Dataset** | 500-row synthetic health dataset with realistic distributions using NumPy |
| 📖 **Theory + Practical** | Each concept has a short note, infographic diagram, and live Python implementation |
| 🖼️ **Infographic Visuals** | 8 custom classic-styled diagrams for all Part A concepts |
| 🧪 **5 Statistical Tests** | Z-Test, T-Test, Chi-Square, One-Way ANOVA, Pearson Correlation |
| 📊 **95% Confidence Intervals** | Computed for Age, Weight, BMI, Blood Pressure, Cholesterol, Glucose |
| 📋 **Summary Table** | Final Accept/Reject H₀ decision table covering all tests |
| 🎨 **Clean Code** | Minimal, readable code cells — no verbose patterns |

---

## 🗂️ Dataset Structure

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="820"/>
</p>

| 🏷️ Field | 🔢 Type | 📝 Description |
|---------|--------|---------------|
| 🆔 `record_id` | String | Unique UUID identifier for each record |
| 👶 `age` | Int | Age of the individual |
| 🗂️ `age_group` | String | Grouped category: 18-25, 26-35, 36-45, 46-60, 60+ |
| ⚖️ `weight` | Float | Weight of individual (kg) |
| 🚻 `gender` | String | Male / Female / Other |
| 🗺️ `region` | String | North / South / East / West |
| 🚬 `smoking_status` | String | Smoker / Non-Smoker / Former Smoker |
| 🏃 `exercise_frequency` | String | Daily / Weekly / Rarely / Never |
| 📐 `bmi` | Float | Body Mass Index |
| 💉 `blood_pressure` | Float | Systolic blood pressure (mmHg) |
| 🩸 `diabetes` | Boolean | Whether individual has diabetes (True/False) |
| 🫀 `hypertension` | Boolean | Whether individual has hypertension (True/False) |
| 🧪 `cholesterol_level` | Float | Total cholesterol level (mg/dL) |
| 🍬 `glucose_level` | Float | Fasting glucose level (mg/dL) |
| 📅 `visit_date` | Date | Date of health check-up or diagnosis |

---

## 📁 Project Structure

```
📦 Derivable-Judgement/
│
├── 📓 derivable_judgement.ipynb     ← Main notebook (Theory + Practical)
├── 📄 README.md                     ← Project documentation (this file)
│
├── 📊 Part A — Theory Cells
│   ├── 1️⃣  Inferential Statistics     ← Concept note + infographic
│   ├── 2️⃣  Hypothesis Testing         ← Step-by-step flow diagram
│   ├── 3️⃣  Confidence Interval        ← Normal curve with CI zones
│   ├── 4️⃣  P-Value                    ← Reject vs Fail-to-Reject panels
│   ├── 5️⃣  Type I & II Errors         ← Error matrix diagram
│   ├── 6️⃣  Statistical Tests          ← 4 distribution cards
│   ├── 7️⃣  Covariance                 ← 3 scatter plots
│   └── 8️⃣  Correlation                ← 6 scatter plots + scale bar
│
└── 🔬 Part B — Code Cells
    ├── 📦  Dataset Generation
    ├── 📐  Confidence Intervals
    ├── 🔬  Z-Test & T-Test
    ├── 📊  Chi-Square Test
    ├── 📈  One-Way ANOVA
    ├── 🔗  Covariance & Correlation
    └── 📋  Final Summary Table
```

---

## 🔁 Project Workflow

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="820"/>
</p>

---

## 📖 Part A — Theory & Concepts

> 💬 All 8 statistical concepts are explained with **plain-language notes**, **mathematical formulas**, and **custom infographic diagrams** — no code in this section.

---

### 1️⃣ Inferential Statistics

> 💬 **In simple words:** Instead of surveying every person in a country, we survey 500 people and use mathematics to draw conclusions about everyone. That's inferential statistics — making big conclusions from small samples.

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="800"/>
</p>

**Key idea:** We observe the Sample → compute Statistics → infer about the Population.

$$\bar{x} \xrightarrow{\text{inference}} \mu \qquad s \xrightarrow{\text{inference}} \sigma$$

| 📊 Descriptive | 🔬 Inferential |
|---------------|---------------|
| Summarizes observed data | Makes predictions about population |
| Mean, median, std dev | Hypothesis tests, confidence intervals |
| No uncertainty involved | Uses probability and confidence levels |

---

### 2️⃣ Hypothesis Testing

> 💬 **In simple words:** Before doing an experiment, you make a claim like "smoking has no effect on diabetes." Then you collect data and use math to check — is your claim believable, or should you reject it? That's hypothesis testing.

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="800"/>
</p>

| 🧩 Component | 📝 Meaning | 💡 Example |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| 🎯 **H₀ (Null Hypothesis)** | Default claim — no effect | Smoking has NO effect on diabetes |
| 🔍 **H₁ (Alternative)** | What we want to prove | Smoking DOES affect diabetes |
| 📐 **Test Statistic** | Computed value from sample | z = 2.45 |
| ⚖️ **Significance Level α** | Threshold for rejection | α = 0.05 (5%) |
| 📉 **P-value** | Prob of result under H₀ | p = 0.01 |
| ✅ **Decision Rule** | Reject H₀ if p < α | 0.01 < 0.05 → Reject H₀ |

$$\text{Reject } H_0 \iff p\text{-value} < \alpha$$

---

### 3️⃣ Confidence Interval & Critical Value

> 💬 **In simple words:** If the true average BMI of the population is a dart on a board, the Confidence Interval is the circle drawn around where your dart landed. A 95% CI means: if you threw 100 darts, 95 of your circles would contain the true target.

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="800"/>
</p>

$$CI = \bar{x} \pm z^* \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

| 🎯 Confidence Level | 🔢 Critical Value (z*) |
|--------------------|----------------------|
| 90% | ±1.645 |
| **95%** | **±1.96** |
| 99% | ±2.576 |

---

### 4️⃣ P-Value

> 💬 **In simple words:** The p-value asks "If H₀ were really true, how surprising would my data be?" A tiny p-value (like 0.002) means your data is VERY surprising under H₀ — so you reject it. A large p-value means your data is not surprising at all.

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="800"/>
</p>

| 📉 P-value | ✅ Decision |
|-----------|-----------|
| p < 0.05 | **Reject H₀** — Statistically significant |
| p ≥ 0.05 | **Fail to Reject H₀** — Not enough evidence |

> ⚠️ The p-value does **NOT** tell you the probability that H₀ is true — it only measures how surprising your sample result is, assuming H₀ were true.

---

### 5️⃣ Type I & Type II Errors

> 💬 **In simple words:** Imagine a smoke alarm. A Type I error is the alarm going off when there's no fire (false alarm). A Type II error is the alarm staying silent during a real fire (missed detection). Both are bad — you can't eliminate both without a bigger, better sensor (larger sample size).

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="800"/>
</p>

$$P(\text{Type I Error}) = \alpha \qquad P(\text{Type II Error}) = \beta \qquad \text{Power} = 1 - \beta$$

| ❌ Error Type | 📝 What Happens | 🎛️ Controlled By |
|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Type I (False Positive)** | Reject H₀ when it's actually true | Significance level α |
| **Type II (False Negative)** | Fail to reject H₀ when it's actually false | Sample size n |

---

### 6️⃣ Statistical Tests

> 💬 **In simple words:** Different situations need different tools. Z-test is like a large ruler (big samples), T-test is a smaller precise ruler (small samples), Chi-Square checks if two categories are related, and ANOVA compares multiple groups at once.

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="800"/>
</p>

| 🧪 Test | 📋 When to Use | 📐 Formula | 🔍 In This Project |
|--------|--------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Z-Test** | n > 30, σ known | $z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ | BMI: Diabetic vs Non-Diabetic |
| **T-Test** | n < 30 or σ unknown | $t = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p\sqrt{1/n_1 + 1/n_2}}$ | BMI: Diabetic vs Non-Diabetic |
| **Chi-Square** | Categorical data | $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O-E)^2}{E}$ | Smoking vs Diabetes |
| **ANOVA** | 3+ group means | $F = \frac{\text{Between-group variance}}{\text{Within-group variance}}$ | BP across Age Groups |

---

### 7️⃣ Covariance

> 💬 **In simple words:** Covariance asks "when one variable goes up, does the other go up too?" If you earn more money and you also spend more money — that's positive covariance. If you exercise more and your weight goes down — that's negative covariance.

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="800"/>
</p>

$$Cov(X,Y) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{n - 1}$$

| 📈 Value | 💡 Meaning |
|---------|-----------|
| **Cov > 0** | Both variables increase together |
| **Cov < 0** | One increases, the other decreases |
| **Cov ≈ 0** | No consistent linear relationship |

> ⚠️ Covariance magnitude depends on variable **units** — making direct comparisons hard. Use Correlation for that.

---

### 8️⃣ Correlation

> 💬 **In simple words:** Correlation is covariance with the units removed. It always stays between -1 and +1. A correlation of +0.9 means almost perfectly in sync. A correlation of -0.9 means almost perfectly opposite. A correlation near 0 means they don't move together at all.

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="800"/>
</p>

$$r = \frac{Cov(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \times \sigma_Y} \qquad -1 \leq r \leq +1$$

| 📊 r Value | 🔍 Interpretation |
|-----------|-----------------|
| r = ±1.0 | Perfect linear relationship |
| ±0.7 to ±1.0 | Strong relationship |
| ±0.3 to ±0.7 | Moderate relationship |
| −0.3 to +0.3 | Weak or no linear relationship |

---

## 🔬 Part B — Analysis Tasks

| # | 🧪 Task | 🛠️ Method | 📋 Hypothesis |
|---|--------|----------|--------------|
| 1️⃣ | Hypothesis Formulation | Manual statement | 3 hypotheses defined |
| 2️⃣ | Confidence Intervals | `scipy.stats.norm.ppf` | 95% CI for 5 variables |
| 3️⃣ | Critical Values | `scipy.stats` | Z, T, Chi², F critical values |
| 4️⃣ | Z-Test + T-Test | Manual + `ttest_ind` | BMI: Diabetic vs Non-Diabetic |
| 5️⃣ | Chi-Square Test | `chi2_contingency` | Smoking vs Diabetes |
| 6️⃣ | One-Way ANOVA | `f_oneway` | Blood Pressure across Age Groups |
| 7️⃣ | Covariance & Correlation | Manual + `pearsonr` | Age vs BMI |
| 8️⃣ | Summary Table | Print formatting | Accept/Reject H₀ for all tests |

---

## 📊 Key Results

<p align="center">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," width="820"/>
</p>

| 🧪 Test | 📐 Statistic | 📉 P-Value | ✅ Decision |
|--------|------------|-----------|-----------|
| 🔬 Z-Test (BMI: Diabetic vs Non-Diabetic) | ~3.80 | < 0.001 | ✅ **REJECT H₀** |
| 🔬 T-Test (BMI: Diabetic vs Non-Diabetic) | ~3.80 | < 0.001 | ✅ **REJECT H₀** |
| 📊 Chi-Square (Smoking vs Diabetes) | ~18.4 | < 0.001 | ✅ **REJECT H₀** |
| 📈 ANOVA (Blood Pressure × Age Groups) | ~22.1 | < 0.001 | ✅ **REJECT H₀** |
| 🔗 Pearson r (Age vs BMI) | ~0.15 | < 0.05 | ✅ **Significant** |

> 💬 **What do these results mean?**
> - 🩸 **Diabetic individuals have significantly higher BMI** than non-diabetic ones
> - 🚬 **Smoking status is statistically associated** with Diabetes prevalence
> - 💓 **Blood pressure increases significantly** with age across all 5 age groups
> - 📐 **Age and BMI have a weak positive correlation** — older people tend to have slightly higher BMI

---

## 🛠️ Technologies Used

| 🔧 Tool | 📝 Purpose |
|--------|-----------|
| 🐍 **Python 3.x** | Core programming language |
| 📓 **Jupyter Notebook** | Interactive development & presentation |
| 🔢 **NumPy** | Synthetic dataset generation, mathematical operations |
| 🐼 **Pandas** | Data manipulation, crosstab, groupby |
| 🔬 **SciPy** | Z-test, T-test, Chi-Square, ANOVA, Pearson r |
| 📊 **Matplotlib** | All charts, infographic diagrams |

---

## ⚙️ Installation

```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/Dhairyapatel1mc/Derivable-Judgement.git
cd Derivable-Judgement

# 2. Install required libraries
pip install numpy pandas scipy matplotlib jupyter
```

---

## ▶️ How to Run

```bash
# Launch Jupyter Notebook
jupyter notebook derivable_judgement.ipynb
```

> 💬 Run all cells top to bottom. The dataset is generated automatically in the first cell — no external CSV file needed.

---

## 🏆 Learning Outcomes

- 🎯 Understood the difference between **descriptive and inferential statistics**
- 📐 Learned to compute and interpret **confidence intervals** and **critical values**
- 🧪 Applied **four different statistical tests** to real-world-style health data
- 📊 Built and interpreted a **contingency table** with chi-square analysis
- 🔗 Computed **covariance and Pearson correlation** both manually and using SciPy
- 🖼️ Designed **professional infographic diagrams** using Matplotlib for concept visualization
- 📋 Practised making clear **statistical decisions** — Accept or Reject H₀ — with written interpretation

---

## 🚀 Future Improvements

- 🔮 Add **logistic regression** to model diabetes probability from health features
- 🗺️ Include **region-wise analysis** using grouped bar charts and chi-square tests
- 📡 Connect to a **real public health API** (e.g. WHO, CDC) instead of synthetic data
- 📱 Build an interactive **Streamlit dashboard** for live hypothesis testing
- 🔁 Add **post-hoc tests** (Tukey HSD) after ANOVA for pairwise group comparison
- 📦 Package as a **reusable statistical analysis module** for other health datasets

---
